data.title: U.S. dollar slides as markets reassess Fed path and geopolitical risks: Currency Recap
data.description:
The U.S. dollar was soft on Tuesday, extending recent losses as political uncertainty, intervention risks, and shifting rate expectations kept pressure on the greenback.

The dollar index was last down 1.30% at $95.78, and is lower by around 2% so far this year.

Dollar weekly moves and key drivers:

Over the past week, the U.S. Dollar Index (DXY), which measures the dollar against a basket of six major currencies, fell 1.78%, as markets priced in growing geopolitical risks, speculation around currency intervention and caution ahead of the Federal Reserve’s policy meeting.

 

Political developments remained a key driver, with heightened focus on U.S. trade tensions and renewed debate over dollar policy following President Donald Trump’s remarks that the currency was “doing great” despite its recent slide, while investors digested developments from Greenland-related trade concerns, ongoing tariff risks, and wider political uncertainty influencing risk sentiment. 

Analysts also pointed to rising intervention risks in USD/JPY, while Bank of America flagged increasing market preference for dollar selling amid geopolitical uncertainty.

The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) fell one basis point to 4.25%, while the 2-year yield (US2Y) slipped one basis point to 3.59%.

Currency Highlights:

Major currency movements (January 19 to January 26)

Euro (EUR:USD) +1.78%
Pound Sterling (GBP:USD) +1.74%
Japanese Yen (JPY:USD) +2.21%
Chinese Yuan (CNY:USD) +0.01%
Swiss Franc (CHF:USD) +2.71%
Australian Dollar (AUD:USD) +2.58%
Canadian Dollar (CAD:USD) +1.07%

The Japanese yen (JPY:USD) was last up 1.29%, as markets remained alert to the risk of official intervention after last week’s sharp rally revived speculation of coordinated action by Japanese authorities, possibly with U.S. backing.

The euro (EUR:USD) was last up 1.26%, supported by improved global risk sentiment and signs of stabilizing economic activity in the euro area, as recent PMI readings showed steady composite growth and a modest pickup in manufacturing, helping European equities extend gains despite lingering geopolitical tensions.

The British pound (GBP:USD) was last up 1.13%, finding support as UK data pointed to resilient economic momentum, with January PMI readings showing broad-based expansion across manufacturing and services, alongside a stronger-than-expected rise in December retail sales. Sterling also tracked improved global risk sentiment as European equities rebounded, despite lingering uncertainty around geopolitics and trade rhetoric.

data.title: Pfizer, Novartis, Lilly drugs in latest round of Medicare drug price negotiations
data.description:
• Xeljanz (tofacitinib), Cosentyx (secukinumab), and Trulicity (dulaglutide), manufactured by, respectively, Pfizer (PFE), Novartis (NVS), and Eli Lilly (LLY), are among the 15 latest drugs chosen by the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services for Medicare Part D price negotiations.
  • Other drugs are AbbVie's (ABBV) Botox (onabotulinumtoxinA), Bristol Myers Squibb's (BMY) Orencia (abatacept), and Gilead Sciences' (GILD)  Biktarvy (bictegravir/emtricitabine/tenofovir).
  • CMS said that between November 2024 and October 2025, about 1.8M individuals with Part D or Medicare Part B coverage used the 15 drugs on the list accounting for $27B in prescription drug spending.
  • The negotiated prices will go into effect in 2028.
  • The complete list of the 15 drugs is available here.

data.title: Camden National outlines low to mid-single-digit deposit growth for 2026 while expanding digital engagement
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Camden National Corporation (CAC) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Simon Griffiths, CEO, opened the call by highlighting "fourth quarter earnings of $22.6 million, representing yet another record-setting achievement for the organization" and emphasized a "6% increase in earnings from the prior quarter." He noted "13 basis points of net interest margin expansion over the prior quarter to 3.29% and a non-GAAP efficiency ratio below 52% and a return on average assets of 1.3%." Griffiths described the Northway Financial acquisition as "now fully delivering," putting the company "ahead of our strategic and financial objectives in several areas." He said, "we are accelerating organic growth through a broader commercial footprint in our southern markets, continued expansion of retail products and digital capabilities across the franchise and deeper leverage of the strength of our wealth and brokerage franchise."
  • The CEO reported "15% organic growth of assets under administration to $2.4 billion at December 31, 2025." In digital, he introduced "family wallet, a no fee parent-controlled youth banking platform," and cited a "19% year-over-year increase in digital engagement among customers under the age of 45, as measured by monthly logins."
  • He addressed credit quality: "Nonperforming assets as of December 31, 2025, were 10 basis points of total assets, and total past due loans were 16 basis points of total loans."
  • Michael Archer, CFO, stated, "we are very pleased with our finish to the year, reporting net income of $22.6 million and diluted earnings per share of $1.33 for the fourth quarter. Net income of $65.2 million and diluted earnings per share of $3.84 for the year ended December 31, 2025."
  • Archer continued, "Net interest income increased 5% between quarters, driven by a 13 basis point expansion in net interest margin to 3.29% in the fourth quarter. Funding costs between quarters decreased 11 basis points to 1.79% in the fourth quarter as we've been able to successfully manage deposit costs following the most recent Fed rate cuts."
  • He also announced, "In early January, we announced a new share repurchase program that gives us the ability to repurchase up to 850,000 shares of the company's common stock or approximately 5% of shares currently outstanding."

Outlook

  • Management said, "we anticipate our first quarter of 2026 deposit balances will be relatively flat with the fourth quarter despite normal seasonality in our deposit base during the winter months, given the impact of recent deposit relationship wins across our sales teams."
  • Archer provided operating expense guidance: "We currently estimate our first quarter operating expenses will range from $36 million to $37 million."
  • For noninterest income, Archer said, "we currently estimate noninterest income will range from $12 million to $13 million for the first quarter of 2026."
  • On loan growth, Griffiths noted, "We expect loan growth this quarter, as Mike indicated, to be sort of flat to up 2%. But certainly, as we get into the rest of the year, we see a pickup in that April, May time frame and certainly mid, mid-single digits is certainly our outlook."
  • Griffiths projected deposit growth in 2026: "We see low to mid-single-digit growth this year."

Financial Results

  • Archer reported, "In the fourth quarter, we again saw strong revenue growth, up 4% over the third quarter."
  • He added, "average loan growth of 1% and average deposit growth of 2% and higher fair value mark accretion of $735,000, which was driven by elevated payoffs on acquired loans."
  • Archer specified, "Noninterest income for the fourth quarter totaled $14.1 million," and noted, "Reported noninterest expense for the fourth quarter was $36.9 million."
  • A provision for credit losses was recorded: "For the fourth quarter, we reported a provision for credit losses of $3 million, driven by the single charge-off Simon mentioned earlier. As of December 31, our loan loss reserve totaled $45.3 million, which was 91 basis points of total loans."

Q&A

  • Stephen Moss, Raymond James: Asked about margin trends and deposit costs after Fed rate cuts. Archer responded, "we're kind of in a couple of basis points here for the first quarter of core margin expansion...on the funding cost side, we do see that continued improvement there, I would say, in the neighborhood of 7 to 10 basis points potentially for the quarter."
  • Moss inquired about loan growth pipeline. Griffiths replied, "residential pipeline is just over $83 million. Commercial pipeline is just over $77 million...We expect loan growth this quarter...to be sort of flat to up 2%."
  • Moss asked about capital deployment with the share buyback. Archer said, "our focus right now continues just to be to return capital, continue to build, but certainly, we'll be opportunistic on leveraging the repurchase program."
  • Damon Del Monte, KBW: Asked about fair value accretion. Archer answered, "In total, it was $5.3 million, I believe, for the quarter."
  • Del Monte asked about commercial hires. Griffiths said, "We certainly continue to look for talent, particularly in the key markets. We've had a couple of really nice hires recently."
  • Daniel Cardenas, Janney: Questioned deposit growth keeping pace with lending. Griffiths stated, "We see low to mid-single-digit growth this year."
  • Cardenas asked about operating expenses. Archer responded, "I think from an efficiency ratio...mid-50s is probably a good spot and normal for us."
  • Cardenas asked about tax rate. Archer said, "I think we'll be a little bit higher from an effective tax rate perspective...maybe sneak up 1%."
  • Matthew Breese, Stephens: Asked about an office CRE charge-off. Griffiths explained, "we were successful in negotiating a deal that provided 88% recovery on the loan...office represents 3.7% of the entire portfolio and is an extremely good condition."
  • Breese asked about CD repricing. Archer said, "about 40% of our CDs are repricing...at a blended rate at right around 3.35%."
  • Breese inquired about securities. Archer replied, "our cash flow continues to be pretty steady...$10 million, $11 million, call it, a month."
  • Breese asked about M&A. Griffiths responded, "very much a continued path for us, very focused on organic growth...On the M&A side, we continue to be opportunistic."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were neutral to slightly positive, seeking clarity on margin expansion, loan growth, deposit costs, and risk management, with questions focusing on practical drivers rather than expressing skepticism or concern.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, emphasizing operational momentum and strategic progress. Griffiths stated, "we remain confident in the overall health of our well-diversified loan portfolio," and Archer said, "we are very pleased with our finish to the year."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management maintained a consistent, positive sentiment, with management slightly more assertive following the completion of the Northway integration and a record year-end.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from a focus on capital rebuilding post-acquisition in Q3 to acceleration in organic growth and digital expansion in Q4.
  • The current quarter highlighted successful Northway integration, increased digital engagement, and a new share repurchase program. Previous quarter emphasized tangible book value accretion, capital deployment, and risk management post-syndicated loan charge-off.
  • Key metrics evolved positively: net interest margin expanded further, efficiency ratio improved, and digital engagement accelerated.
  • Analyst questions in both quarters focused on loan growth, margins, and risk but with more interest in deposit trends and digital products in Q4.
  • Management confidence increased, particularly regarding digital strategy, deposit growth, and the loan portfolio's quality.

Risks and Concerns

  • Griffiths discussed credit actions: "During the fourth quarter, we had the opportunity to complete a short sale on a commercial real estate office loan...resulting in a $3 million charge-off and an 88% recovery of the loan balance."
  • Archer addressed provision levels: "we continue to believe we have sufficient loan loss reserves set aside given the strength and historical performance of our loan portfolio."
  • Analysts asked about the potential normalization of provision levels and credit quality, with Archer stating, "we feel pretty good around the 90, 91 basis points on ACL to loans ratio."
  • No significant concerns about portfolio risk or capital were raised, and management described proactive credit management and strong portfolio metrics.

Final Takeaway

Management expressed confidence in its strategy, digital expansion, and disciplined risk management, stating that Camden National enters 2026 in a position of strength with a robust balance sheet, a growing digital platform, and a focus on organic growth in key markets. The completion of the Northway acquisition and the launch of new digital products support the company's outlook for continued loan and deposit growth, efficient operations, and ongoing shareholder returns through the newly authorized share repurchase program.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Alexandria outlines $2.9B 2026 dispositions target and expects occupancy dip in Q1 2026 as portfolio strategy intensifies
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Alexandria Real Estate Equities (ARE) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Founder & Executive Chairman Joel Marcus stated that "2026 is all about timely execution of our plan, heavily focused on dispositions and maintaining a strong and flexible balance sheet, driving occupancy with intense leasing focus on vacant space, rollover space and redevelopment and development space and meeting the marketplace." Marcus also highlighted a shift toward significantly reducing CapEx and noted the company's Investor Day plan as the "North Star for 2026."
  • CFO Marc Binda congratulated the team on completing $1.5 billion of dispositions across 26 transactions and achieving 1.2 million square feet of leasing volume in the quarter, "the highest quarter in the last year." Binda reported "FFO per share diluted as adjusted was $2.16 for 4Q '25 and $9.01 for the year, which represents the midpoint of our prior guidance provided on our last quarterly call." He emphasized that leasing of vacant space reached 393,000 rentable square feet, almost double the quarterly average over the last five quarters. Occupancy ended 2025 at 90.9%.
  • Binda noted, "We reiterated our year-end 2026 occupancy range of 87.7% to 89.3% that was provided at our Investor Day this past December." He also announced nearly 900,000 rentable square feet of leases expected to commence in Q3 2026, generating $52 million incremental annual rental revenue.
  • The company reaffirmed its focus on large-scale non-core asset dispositions and highlighted a plan to reduce the size of its land bank, with non-core assets and land expected to comprise 65% to 75% of the $2.9 billion midpoint 2026 disposition target.

Outlook

  • The company reiterated its year-end 2026 occupancy guidance of 87.7% to 89.3% and expects occupancy to "dip in the first quarter of 2026, and we expect occupancy growth in the second half of 2026" (Binda).
  • Same-property net operating income performance for 2026 is projected to be "up/down 8.5% at the midpoint of our guidance range, which is expected to be driven by lower occupancy."
  • Capitalized interest for 2026 is guided at $250 million, a 24% decrease from 2025.
  • The guidance assumes completion of the nearly 900,000 square feet of signed leases commencing in Q3 2026 and a 2% to 3% benefit from asset sales or held-for-sale designations in the second half of 2026.
  • The company reaffirmed its 2026 FFO per share diluted as adjusted guidance and leverage range for Q4 2026 net debt to annualized adjusted EBITDA of 5.6x to 6.2x.

Financial Results

  • The company reported FFO per share diluted as adjusted at $2.16 for Q4 2025 and $9.01 for the full year.
  • Leasing volume for Q4 was 1.2 million square feet, with leasing of vacant space at 393,000 rentable square feet.
  • Occupancy at the end of 2025 was 90.9%, up 30 basis points from the prior quarter.
  • Realized gains from venture investments were $21 million for the quarter.
  • General and administrative cost savings of $51.3 million (30%) were achieved in 2025 compared to the prior year.
  • The company recognized $1.45 billion in impairments in Q4, with 50% to 60% related to land and the two largest impairments comprising 37% of the total.
  • Liquidity stood at $5.3 billion, and the average remaining debt maturity was just over 12 years.

Q&A

  • Farrell Granath, BofA Securities: Asked about how improved VC funding and broader market sentiment are translating to leasing. Hallie Kuhn explained that "decision-making is still taking longer and companies are very cautious in terms of how they think about taking on new space or expanding." Marcus noted a "very small amount of leasing for public biotech" in the fourth quarter and expressed hope for improvement in 2026.
  • Ronald Kamdem, Morgan Stanley: Inquired about cap rate trends and leasing pipeline. Peter M. Moglia said, "cap rates for those in the mid 6s, all the way up to the mid-9s" and noted that "smaller space is under 50,000 square feet are still what is moving most of the tours."
  • John Kim, BMO: Sought confirmation on 4Q '26 FFO guidance and trough earnings. Binda responded, "we're still tracking within that range that we gave for the fourth quarter of '26, which I think was $1.40 to $1.60."
  • Vikram Malhotra, Mizuho: Asked about the earnings trajectory and incentive packages. Binda confirmed Q4 as the low point for earnings in 2026. Moglia stated, "tenant improvements haven't changed. They're still elevated for anything that's from shell," but "where we continue to see weakening in fundamentals is in the free rent category, and it's continued to elevate."
  • Richard Anderson, Cantor Fitzgerald: Probed on stock performance and investor base. Marcus reflected, "the nature of investors change over time" and highlighted the diversity of investor interest. Anderson also asked about development exposure, to which Marcus replied, "we've hypothesized that we think 10%, somewhere 10-plus percent as a percentage of nonproductive or non-income-producing land as a percentage of overall gross assets is probably where we want to be."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a cautious tone, frequently probing for clarity on leasing drivers, incentive packages, and the company’s ability to execute dispositions. Multiple questions focused on the cadence of earnings, cap rates, and occupancy trends, reflecting skepticism about the pace of recovery and durability of recent momentum.
  • Management’s tone during prepared remarks was focused and confident, reiterating guidance and outlining strategy. In the Q&A, management adopted a measured, sometimes cautiously optimistic tone, as seen in repeated references to "cautiously optimistic" and the need to "monitor closely". Phrases such as "we are confident..." and "we expect..." indicated assurance, but repeated acknowledgments of ongoing market volatility and elevated caution around leasing revealed underlying conservatism.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s confidence in achieving disposition targets and maintaining balance sheet strength was more pronounced, but both analysts and management remained wary of macro and industry risks.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter emphasized accelerated dispositions, with $1.5 billion completed in Q4 and a $2.9 billion target for 2026, compared to a more cautious tone on asset sales in the previous quarter.
  • Management reiterated the strategic shift to "timely execution" on asset sales and intense leasing focus, while in the previous quarter, the emphasis was on navigating macro and policy uncertainty.
  • Occupancy improved slightly to 90.9% from 90.6%, and leasing of vacant space nearly doubled the prior five-quarter average.
  • The company maintained guidance for occupancy and FFO but acknowledged a projected Q1 2026 occupancy dip due to lease expirations, a more explicit warning than in the prior quarter.
  • Analysts in both quarters pressed on earnings trajectory, capital allocation, and the impact of supply/demand imbalances, but current quarter questions reflected more focus on leasing incentives and disposition progress.
  • Management’s statements signaled greater urgency on cost controls and balance sheet flexibility.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited "1.2 million square feet of key lease expirations with expected downtime" in Q1 2026 and a "reduction of rent of approximately $6 million per quarter, starting in the first quarter of 2026 related to potential tenant wind-downs."
  • Free rent and rent concessions are under pressure in leasing negotiations, and "fundamentals are in the free rent category, and it's continued to elevate."
  • The company recognized $1.45 billion in impairments, primarily related to land in oversupplied submarkets.
  • Management is evaluating business strategy for several projects under construction and may pause or sell depending on market conditions.
  • Analyst concerns focused on the ability to execute disposals at favorable cap rates, the pace of recovery in public biotech demand, and the risk that further occupancy declines could impact earnings and leverage targets.

Final Takeaway

Alexandria Real Estate Equities underscored its commitment to executing a substantial $2.9 billion disposition program in 2026, while maintaining a disciplined focus on leasing and cost control. Management reiterated that occupancy will dip in early 2026 before improving in the back half of the year, and highlighted the ongoing pressure on rents and incentives amid a challenging market. The company continues to prioritize balance sheet strength and liquidity, with a clear strategy to reduce exposure to non-core and non-income-producing assets, supported by a high-quality tenant base and robust pipeline of signed leases set to commence later in 2026.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Crane signals 10% adjusted EPS growth target for 2026 with robust M&A-driven expansion
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Crane Company (CR) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Max Mitchell, Chairman, President & CEO, highlighted a strong finish to 2025 and an optimistic outlook for 2026, stating, "Our performance last year and our initial guidance for 2026 show that we are consistently and reliably delivering on our commitments and our long-term value creation thesis. 4% to 6% core sales growth, and we were just at the high end of that last year, 35% to 40% core operating leverage and upside from capital deployment." He emphasized the acquisition of Druck, Panametrics, and Reuter-Stokes, noting, "Having closed on the acquisition of these brands on January 1st... Reuter-Stokes doubles the size of our nuclear business, adding industry-leading radiation sensing and detecting technologies." Mitchell also announced the acquisition of optek-Danulat, a leader in optical sensing measurement solutions, and the planned CEO succession, with Alex Alcala set to become CEO on April 27, 2026, while he transitions to Executive Chairman.

  • Alejandro Alcala, Executive VP & COO and incoming CEO, commented, "Looking ahead, we will stay true to our journey, driving the Crane business system to deliver strong organic growth while also pursuing our strategy of accelerated inorganic growth." He described robust segment performance, particularly at Aerospace & Advanced Technologies, and detailed the integration and anticipated accretion of recent acquisitions. Alcala added, "The integration process is off to a strong start, and our outlook for these businesses is already exceeding our initial expectations."

  • Richard Maue, Executive VP & CFO, stated, "We drove 5.4% Core Sales growth in the quarter, reflecting the ongoing strength within the Aerospace & Advanced Technologies segment. Adjusted operating profit increased 16%, reflecting the impact of higher productivity and favorable pricing net of inflation." Maue also noted that the company closed the year with net leverage of 1.1x after acquisitions and highlighted a 102% adjusted free cash conversion in 2025.

Outlook

  • Initial 2026 adjusted EPS guidance is $6.55 to $6.75, representing approximately 10% adjusted EPS growth at the midpoint, excluding the $0.16 benefit of one-time hurricane-related insurance recoveries received in 2025 and after-tax acquisition-related intangible amortization in both years. Management expects the recent acquisitions to be slightly accretive to 2026 earnings. Alcala indicated, "Given the level of activity we are seeing for 2026, we expect core sales growth for the year to be up at the high end of our [indiscernible] assumption."

  • The company previewed a cautious view on Process Flow Technologies demand in 2026, expecting flat to low single-digit core growth, while maintaining targeted core leverage.

Financial Results

  • Adjusted EPS for the quarter was $1.53, up 21% over the prior year. Full year adjusted EPS increased 24%. Core sales growth for the quarter reached 5.4%. Aerospace & Advanced Technologies sales were $272 million, up 15%, with a record backlog of just over $1 billion, up 25% year-over-year. OEM sales in the segment increased 23% for the quarter. Adjusted segment margin for Aerospace & Advanced Technologies was 23.6%, up 50 basis points from last year.

  • Process Flow Technologies delivered sales of $309 million, flat year-over-year, with core sales down 1.5% and a 1.6 point favorable FX impact. Adjusted operating margin for PFT was 22%, a 170 basis point increase. The company received $5.2 million in insurance recoveries related to Hurricane Helene in Q4.

Q&A

  • Scott Deuschle, Deutsche Bank, asked about pricing opportunities at Druck and long-term agreements. Alejandro Alcala responded that all three acquired businesses offer significant opportunities for value pricing and operational improvements, with expected improvements at Druck starting in 2026 and accelerating into 2027.

  • Jeffrey Sprague, Vertical Research, probed on cost integration versus cost takeout for the acquisitions. Alcala explained that while there are costs to integrate, the net impact will be cost out, with margin improvements increasing in 2027 and 2028 as actions materialize.

  • Matt Summerville, D.A. Davidson, inquired about Aerospace segment aftermarket expectations and potential government shutdown impacts. Alcala projected continued momentum in commercial and military aftermarket and noted a brief delay in F-16 program flight tests, which is factored into guidance.

  • Amit Mehrotra, UBS, asked about power generation and nuclear exposure. Alcala explained that Reuter-Stokes has doubled Crane’s nuclear business and outlined four primary areas of nuclear growth, including restarts, new construction, small modular reactors, and license extensions.

  • Nathan Jones, Stifel, asked about revenue synergies from acquisitions. Alcala said growth synergies are expected but not included in current guidance, with confidence that these will contribute to the company reaching the high end of its 4% to 6% core sales growth target.

  • Justin Ages, CJS Securities, questioned the timing of international F-16 orders and leverage targets. Alcala clarified that international orders extend the program timeline, while Maue stated the company is comfortable going up to 3x leverage for the right deal.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed for detail on pricing, integration costs, margin progression, and market-specific growth, displaying a slightly positive to neutral tone with ongoing curiosity about the impact of acquisitions.

  • Management maintained a confident, upbeat, and occasionally humorous tone during prepared remarks and Q&A, emphasizing strong execution, seamless integration, and a robust M&A pipeline. Confidence was underscored by phrases such as "I'm very confident we will meet or exceed our targets" (Alcala) and "Our progress speaks for itself, and truly, there's no limit to what we will accomplish in 2026 and beyond" (Mitchell).

  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone remained confident but was more forward-looking regarding M&A accretion and CEO succession, while analysts focused more on integration risks and market cyclicality.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter featured a formal announcement of multiple acquisitions closing, versus the prior quarter’s anticipation of the PSI deal. The CEO succession plan was introduced, marking a significant strategic development not present last quarter.

  • Adjusted EPS guidance for 2026 ($6.55 to $6.75) was provided, compared to last quarter’s raised 2025 guidance of $5.75 to $5.95. The company shifted from discussing organic growth to a greater emphasis on accretion and integration from M&A.

  • Analysts in both quarters questioned chemical market trends and margin drivers, but in Q4 became more focused on integration costs and revenue synergy realization post-acquisitions.

  • Management’s confidence level remained high, with an enhanced focus on growth through acquisitions and organizational transition, while analysts’ tone was steady but increasingly inquisitive regarding execution risks.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited sluggish chemical end markets and a cautious near-term outlook for Process Flow Technologies as challenges. Alcala noted, "Looking ahead to 2026 for PFT, given our fourth quarter orders remain sluggish, we are adopting a cautious view of 2026 demand levels to start the year."

  • Integration of multiple acquisitions presents execution risk, as acknowledged in discussions about cost in versus cost out.

  • There is near-term margin dilution expected from newly acquired businesses, though growth and synergy realization are projected over time.

  • Analysts raised concerns regarding timing of revenue synergies, impact of government shutdowns on defense programs, and the trajectory of margin improvement.

Final Takeaway

Crane Company enters 2026 with strong momentum, underpinned by robust organic growth, strategic acquisitions, and a leadership transition designed to sustain long-term value creation. Management projects 10% adjusted EPS growth at the midpoint for 2026, driven by core sales gains and the accretive integration of Druck, Panametrics, Reuter-Stokes, and optek-Danulat. While near-term challenges persist in certain end markets and integration costs are anticipated, the company remains confident in its ability to deliver above-market growth, improve margins over time, and capitalize on a growing pipeline of M&A opportunities.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Trump talks Deere investment, tractor regulations, E15 deal
data.description:
John Deere (DE) is investing $70M in an excavator factory in North Carolina, President Donald Trump said on Tuesday in Iowa, adding that it will be the only excavator made entirely in the U.S.

During the rally in Clive, Iowa, Trump repeated his claim that he has commitments for $18T of investments in the U.S. That's more than half the size of the current $31.8T U.S. GDP.

He said he'll reduce some regulations applying to tractors, "so you don't need a PhD to start your tractor."

Furthermore, Trump said he's working on a deal for higher-ethanol E15 gasoline blends. "I would sign a bill on it without delay," he said. That's meaningful for Iowan corn farmers, who supply grain for ethanol.

One year after Trump's second term started, "our income is booming, wages are rising, investment is soaring," he said.

"This is the most dramatic one-year turnaround in history," he claimed, crediting the country's growth to the tariffs he implemented.

"Economic growth is exploding in numbers unheard of," Trump added. In Q3, U.S. GDP increased by 4.4%, on an annualized basis, which came after 3.8% growth in Q2 and -0.6% in Q1.

Since the presidential election, there have been 52 all-time stock market highs, adding $9T to 401(k)s, retirement plans, and investment accounts, Trump said.

He asked the crowd whether he should run for president a fourth time, even though the Constitution limits any person to two terms as president.

In a statement posted earlier on Tuesday, the Trump administration said Iowa's average gas price has declined to $2.56 per gallon, with some offering gas at under $2 per gallon. The administration has saved the average Iowa family $3,139 in taxes this year, and improved home affordability, with the median home price in Iowa down 3.5% from a year ago. Furthermore, "Iowa's economy is roaring ahead, with strong GDP growth outpacing the national average, fueled by agriculture, manufacturing, and smart policies," it said.

"The proof is in the results: Lower costs, rising wages, protected jobs, and renewed strength mean Iowa families are winning again under President Trump — and the best is yet to come," the White House said.

Developing… Check back for updates.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Zefiro Methane reduces debt by $1.79M and adds $0.44M in liquidity
data.description:
• Zefiro Methane (ZEFI:CA) announced on Tuesday it has entered loan satisfaction agreements with three creditors to eliminate $1.79M in debt from a May 2025 secured loan and add about $0.44M in cash to its balance sheet.
  • Pursuant to the loan satisfaction agreements, the creditors will exercise 10.79M warrants, with 75% of the exercise price applied against debt and the remaining 25% paid in cash.
  • The remaining loan principal and accrued interest were settled through the issuance of 2.42M common shares at CAD $0.44 each.
  • The company intends to use $0.44M to retire other near-term debt and for general working capital purposes.
  • Catherine Flax, a Director, and officer of the company, participated in the loan satisfaction agreements, settling $0.8M in principal and $0.14M in interest through the issuance of 1.08M common shares.
  • Shares +3.37%.

data.title: Gold ekes out new record high on dollar weakness, silver slips after historic rally
data.description:
Gold futures squeezed out another record high on Tuesday, with the front-month contract rising for the sixth consecutive session, gaining nearly 11% in that time, as persistent geopolitical risks and a flight from sovereign bonds and currencies extended the metal's rally.

Dollar weakness has been a key factor behind gold's gains, and the greenback fell further on Tuesday after President Trump said he was not concerned with the dollar's decline.

In recent days, Trump also threatened to hike tariffs on South Korean goods, citing what he said was the failure of the country's legislature to codify a trade deal, and threatened to impose 100% tariffs on Canada if the country struck a trade deal with China.

The risk of a partial U.S. government shutdown looms ahead of the January 30 funding deadline, and markets also are focused on the U.S. Federal Reserve's two-day policy meeting, with expectations that the Fed will keep rates unchanged.

The Trump administration's threats to annex Greenland and persistent attacks on the Federal Reserve's independence also have unnerved markets.

Meanwhile, Deutsche Bank and Société Générale now forecast gold prices to reach $6,000/oz by the end of the year.

"Rallies normally end because the drivers that took people into ‍the gold market originally dissipate, and that's just not the case" this time, Bank of America commodities strategist Michael Widmer said in a note.

Front-month Comex gold (XAUUSD:CUR) for January delivery closed up less than 0.1% to $5,079.90/oz, its sixth straight gain, but front-month Comex January silver (XAGUSD:CUR) settled -8.3% to $105.523/oz, the metal's largest one-day dollar drop since January 1980 after gaining 14% on Monday.

ETFs: (GLD), (GDX), (GDXJ), (IAU), (NUGT), (PHYS), (GLDM), (AAAU), (SGOL), (RING), (BAR), (OUNZ), (SLV), (PSLV), (SIVR), (SIL), (SILJ)

data.title: BXP FFO of $1.76 misses by $0.04, revenue of $877.1M beats by $5.09M
data.description:
• BXP press release (BXP): Q4 FFO of $1.76 misses by $0.04.
  • Revenue of $877.1M (+2.2% Y/Y) beats by $5.09M.
  • BXP provided updated guidance for first quarter 2026 EPS of $0.32 - $0.34 and FFO of $1.56 - $1.58 per diluted share, and full year 2026 EPS of $2.08 - $2.29 and FFO of $6.88 - $7.04 per diluted share.
